title: Helicobacter pylori Infections
authors: Emad M. El-Omar, Martin J. Blaser

items.title: Role of H. pylori in Peptic Ulcer Disease and Gastric Malignancies
items.content: H. pylori colonization is the main risk factor for peptic ulceration, gastric adenocarcinoma, and gastric MALT lymphoma. Eradication of H. pylori with antibiotics can provide a permanent cure for peptic ulcers and serves as first-line therapy for low-grade gastric MALT lymphoma.
keyPoints: H. pylori colonizes the stomach in ~40% of the world's human population.
Colonization is the main risk factor for peptic ulceration, gastric adenocarcinoma, and gastric MALT lymphoma.
Antibiotic treatment can eradicate H. pylori, providing a permanent cure for most cases of peptic ulcer disease.
First-line therapy for low-grade gastric MALT lymphoma involves treating H. pylori.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: H. pylori's Protective Role Against GERD Complications
items.content: While H. pylori colonization is a risk factor for several gastric conditions, there is evidence suggesting it may offer protection against complications of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), such as esophageal adenocarcinoma.
keyPoints: H. pylori colonization may protect against GERD complications like esophageal adenocarcinoma.
This protective role is supported by increasing evidence but remains uncertain.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Prevalence Patterns and Risk Factors for Helicobacter pylori Infection
items.content: The prevalence of $H. pylori$ varies significantly by region, with <30% in the United States, Europe, and Oceania, compared to >60% in Africa, South America, and West Asia. In the U.S., 18% of adults and 14% of children are infected. The infection is typically acquired during childhood, influenced by crowding and social deprivation.
keyPoints: Prevalence <30% in US, Europe, Oceania; >60% in Africa, South America, West Asia
U.S. prevalence: 18% adults, 14% children/adolescents
Acquired in childhood due to crowding and social deprivation
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission Routes and Declining Prevalence of Helicobacter pylori
items.content: Humans are the primary reservoir for $H. pylori$, with transmission occurring from parents or peers, more commonly via fecal-oral in less developed countries. The prevalence has declined over recent decades due to socioeconomic improvements and antibacterial treatments.
keyPoints: Humans are the main reservoir
Transmission: parent-to-child (developed) or peer-to-peer (less developed)
Declining prevalence linked to socioeconomic development and antibacterials
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Role of H. pylori Virulence Factors in Disease
items.content: H. pylori virulence factors, particularly the cag island and VacA, are associated with increased risk of peptic ulcer disease and gastric adenocarcinoma.
keyPoints: Cag-positive strains increase risk of peptic ulcer and gastric adenocarcinoma.
VacA forms pores in cell membranes; more active forms increase ulcer and cancer risk.
Adhesins like BabA bind to blood group antigens, contributing to disease.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Host Genetic Factors Influencing H. pylori Disease Risk
items.content: Certain genetic polymorphisms in host immune response genes increase susceptibility to gastric adenocarcinoma in the presence of H. pylori.
keyPoints: Polymorphisms in interleukin 1 gene linked with increased risk of gastric adenocarcinoma.
SNP in prostate stem cell antigen (PSCA) rs2294008 associated with higher gastric cancer risk.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Duodenal Ulceration by H. pylori
items.content: H. pylori-induced changes in the gastric antrum lead to increased gastrin levels and acid secretion, contributing to duodenal ulcer risk.
keyPoints: Reduction in somatostatin-producing D cells leads to higher gastrin levels.
Increased meal-stimulated acid secretion from the gastric corpus is implicated.
Gastric metaplasia in the duodenum may become colonized and inflamed, leading to ulcers.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Gastric Ulcer and Adenocarcinoma
items.content: Gastric ulcers often occur at the junction between antral and corpus-type mucosa, where inflammation is prevalent. Gastric adenocarcinomas typically arise in stomachs with extensive atrophic gastritis and hypo- or achlorhydria, likely due to DNA damage from reactive oxygen and nitrogen species produced by inflammatory cells.
keyPoints: Gastric ulcers commonly occur at the junction of antral and corpus-type mucosa.
Gastric cancer often arises in stomachs with extensive atrophic gastritis and hypo- or achlorhydria.
DNA damage is thought to be due principally to reactive oxygen and nitrogen species from inflammatory cells.
Longitudinal analyses show gastric adenocarcinoma follows changes from simple gastritis to dysplasia.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Gastric MALT Lymphoma
items.content: Low-grade B-cell MALT lymphomas are linked to chronic H. pylori colonization, which stimulates lymphocyte populations. These tumors often respond dramatically to H. pylori eradication therapies. However, the distinction between true malignancy and benign lymphoid proliferation is uncertain.
keyPoints: Reported rate of low-grade B-cell MALT lymphomas was ~1 per million population per year before H. pylori discovery.
CagA-positive H. pylori strains are significantly associated with t(11;18)(q21;q21)-positive gastric MALT lymphoma.
Most responders to H. pylori eradication do not have the characteristic t(11;18) translocation and may represent benign polyclonal proliferation.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: H. pylori's Role in Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: H. pylori colonization is a significant risk factor for peptic ulcer disease, particularly duodenal ulcers (~70%) and gastric ulcers (~50%). Eradication of H. pylori drastically reduces long-term ulcer relapse.
keyPoints: Approximately 10-15% of H. pylori-colonized individuals develop associated illnesses like peptic ulceration.
~70% of duodenal ulcers and ~50% of gastric ulcers are related to H. pylori colonization.
Eradication of H. pylori virtually abolishes long-term ulcer relapse.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.title: H. pylori and Gastric Adenocarcinoma/Lymphoma Risk
items.content: H. pylori colonization is a risk factor for noncardia gastric adenocarcinomas and primary gastric lymphoma, with potential regression of some low-grade B-cell lymphomas upon eradication.
keyPoints: H. pylori colonization is a risk factor for adenocarcinomas of the distal (noncardia) stomach.
Many diagnosed low-grade gastric B-cell lymphomas regress after H. pylori eradication.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.title: H. pylori and Functional Dyspepsia Management
items.content: In patients with functional dyspepsia, H. pylori eradication can lead to symptom resolution more frequently than placebo treatment, though only a small subgroup benefits.
keyPoints: H. pylori eradication leads to symptom resolution up to 15% more commonly than placebo in functional dyspepsia.
Most guidelines recommend H. pylori eradication for patients with persistent and difficult-to-treat functional dyspepsia.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.title: When to Use Endoscopy-Based Tests for H. pylori
items.content: Endoscopy is generally unnecessary in young patients with simple dyspepsia but is useful for older patients or those with alarm symptoms to exclude malignancy and confirm diagnosis.
keyPoints: Biopsy urease test: Detects $H.\ pyori$ by color change due to ammonia production from urea hydrolysis, often within minutes up to 24 hours.
Histologic examination: Requires special stains for optimal visualization of $H.\ pylori$, provides information on inflammation, atrophy, metaplasia, or dysplasia.
Microbiologic culture: Most specific but may be insensitive; confirms identity with Gram's stain and positive oxidase, catalase, urease tests.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Noninvasive Tests for H. pylori Diagnosis
items.content: Noninvasive testing is preferred unless gastric cancer needs exclusion; includes urea breath test, stool antigen test, and serologic assays.
keyPoints: Urea breath test: Patient drinks $^{13}C$-labeled urea solution; detects labeled carbon dioxide in breath if $H.\ pylori$ is present.
Stool antigen test: Uses monoclonal antibodies specific for $H.\ pylori$ antigens, convenient and cost-effective but may be disliked by some patients.
Serologic assays: Measure IgG levels with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay or immunoblot; rapid office tests often lack accuracy.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Assessing Treatment Success for H. pylori Infection
items.content: Urea breath test, stool antigen test, and biopsy-based tests can assess treatment success but are unreliable if performed too soon after therapy.
keyPoints: Tests dependent on $H.\ pylori$ load may yield false negatives <4 weeks post-treatment due to early bacterial suppression.
Noninvasive tests preferred for assessing treatment success; endoscopy recommended post-gastric ulceration to ensure healing and exclude carcinoma.
Serologic tests not suitable for monitoring treatment success as antibody titer drop is too slow, requiring >14 weeks.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Indications for H. pylori Eradication
items.content: H. pylori should be eradicated in patients with documented ulcer disease to prevent relapse, and it is recommended for low-grade gastric B-cell MALT lymphoma. Treatment may also be considered for functional dyspepsia if the patient belongs to the small percentage who benefit beyond placebo effects. In cases of idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, vitamin B12 deficiency, or iron-deficiency anemia where other causes are excluded, eradication is recommended but not universally supported.
keyPoints: Eradicate H. pylori in patients with documented ulcer disease to prevent relapse.
Recommended for low-grade gastric B-cell MALT lymphoma.
Consider treatment for functional dyspepsia if the patient benefits beyond placebo effects.
Eradication may be considered for idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, vitamin B12 deficiency, or iron-deficiency anemia when other causes are excluded.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.title: Endoscopy and Testing Strategy Based on Community Prevalence
items.content: For older dyspeptic patients or those with alarm symptoms, upper gastrointestinal endoscopy is indicated to diagnose and test for H. pylori. In young dyspeptic patients without alarm symptoms, if community prevalence of H. pylori is below ~20%, treat with a short course of acid suppression using a PPI. If nonresponsive or relapse occurs, or if prevalence >20%, testing and eradication are recommended.
keyPoints: Endoscopy indicated for older dyspeptic patients or those with alarm symptoms to diagnose H. pylori.
In young dyspeptics without alarm symptoms and low community prevalence (<~20%), use PPI for acid suppression.
If nonresponsive, relapse occurs, or community prevalence >20%, test and eradicate H. p if found.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.title: Regimens for H. pylori Eradication
items.content: H. pylori eradication requires multidrug regimens due to clinical failure of monotherapy. Current regimens include a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) and two or three antimicrobial agents administered over 10–14 days, with variations based on regional antibiotic resistance patterns.
keyPoints: Multidrug regimens are necessary for H. pylori eradication due to monotherapy failure.
Regimens include a PPI plus two or three antimicrobials for 10–14 days.
Optimal regimens vary by region based on antibiotic resistance.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.question: Which characteristic allows Helicobacter pylori to survive the acidic environment of the stomach?
options: Highly expressed urease that produces buffering ammonia
Spiral shape and flagella for motility
items.explanation: Helicobacter pylori has several acid-resistance mechanisms, most notably a highly expressed urease that catalyzes urea hydrolysis to produce buffering ammonia.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What is the primary habitat of Helicobacter pylori in humans?
items.explanation: Helicobacter pylori lives in gastric mucus, with a proportion of the bacteria adherent to the mucosa.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What is the oxygen requirement for Helicobacter pylori growth?
items.explanation: Helicobacter pylori is microaerophilic, meaning it grows in low levels of oxygen.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of H. pylori infection among adults in the United States?
items.explanation: The passage states that an estimated 18% of adults in the United States are positive for H. pylori.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which factor is most associated with the acquisition of H. pylori during childhood?
options: Markers of crowding and social deprivation
items.explanation: The passage indicates that markers of crowding and social deprivation in childhood are the main risk factors for H. pylori infection.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which regions is the prevalence of H. pylori among adults greater than 60%?
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of H. pylori is greater than 60% in many parts of Africa, South America, and West Asia.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a bacterial virulence factor that increases the risk of peptic ulcer and gastric adenocarcinoma in H. pylori strains?
items.explanation: The cag island encodes a bacterial type IV secretion system that increases the risk of peptic ulcer and gastric adenocarcinoma.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of CagA in H. pylori pathogenesis?
options: It binds to integrins on cell surfaces.
It forms pores in cell membranes.
It induces host cell signal transduction and inflammatory changes.
items.explanation: CagA is an effector protein that, when translocated into epithelial cells, may be activated by phosphorylation to induce host cell signal transduction and inflammatory changes.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which environmental factor increases the risk of gastric cancer in H. pylori-positive individuals?
items.explanation: Smoking is an environmental cofactor that increases the risks of duodenal ulcers and gastric cancer in H. pylori-positive individuals.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What type of gastritis is most closely linked with duodenal ulceration?
items.explanation: Antral-predominant gastritis is most closely linked with duodenal ulceration.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Where do gastric ulcers commonly occur in relation to mucosal types?
options: At the junction of antral and corpus-type mucosa
items.explanation: Gastric ulcers commonly occur at the junction of antral and corpus-type mucosa, where inflammation is often pronounced.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: What condition is associated with gastric cancer arising in stomachs with extensive atrophic gastritis?
items.explanation: Gastric cancer usually arises in stomachs with extensive atrophic gastritis and hypo- or achlorhydria.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: Which type of gastric adenocarcinoma is more common in younger adults without atrophic changes?
items.explanation: The diffuse type of gastric adenocarcinoma is more common in younger adults and may arise directly from chronic gastritis without atrophic changes.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of duodenal ulcers worldwide is related to H. pylori colonization?
items.explanation: Worldwide, ~70% of duodenal ulcers are related to H. pylori colonization.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.question: Which factor is increasingly becoming a cause of gastric ulceration in developed countries?
items.explanation: In many developed countries, aspirin and NSAIDs have overtaken H. pylori as a cause of gastric ulceration.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.question: What is the effect of eradicating H. pylori on long-term ulcer relapse?
items.explanation: Eradication of H. pylori virtually abolishes long-term ulcer relapse.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.question: What is the role of H. pylori in the development of peptic ulcer disease?
options: Neither necessary nor sufficient, but a strong risk factor
items.explanation: H. pylori is neither necessary nor sufficient for the development of peptic ulcer disease, but it is a very strong risk factor.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.question: Which test involves placing gastric biopsy specimens into a gel containing urea and an indicator?
items.explanation: The biopsy urease test involves placing gastric biopsy specimens into a gel containing urea and an indicator.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the primary reason endoscopy is not usually necessary in young patients with simple dyspepsia?
options: To make a positive diagnosis of H. pylori
Because it is commonly used for older patients or those with alarm symptoms
items.explanation: Endoscopy is not usually necessary in young patients with simple dyspepsia because it is commonly used to exclude malignancy and make a positive diagnosis in older patients or those with alarm symptoms.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which noninvasive test uses a solution of urea labeled with the nonradioactive isotope ^13C?
options: Serologic assays measuring specific IgG levels
items.explanation: The urea breath test uses a solution of urea labeled with the nonradioactive isotope ^13C.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which statement about serologic assays for H. pylori is correct?
options: They are used to monitor treatment success.
They measure specific IgG levels in serum by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay or immunoblot.
They are the most accurate diagnostic method available.
They can be performed immediately after antibiotic treatment.
items.explanation: Serologic assays for H. pylori measure specific IgG levels in serum by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay or immunoblot.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is a clear-cut indication for H. pylori eradication?
options: Iron-deficiency anemia with other causes excluded
items.explanation: $H.\ pylori$-related duodenal or gastric ulceration is a clear-cut indication for treatment to prevent relapse.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.question: What is the recommended approach for older dyspeptic patients with alarm symptoms?
options: Immediate eradication of $H.\ pylori$ without testing
Short course of acid suppression using a PPI
No treatment unless ulcers are confirmed
items.explanation: Older dyspeptic patients with alarm symptoms should undergo upper gastrointestinal endoscopy to seek diagnosis and test for $H.\ pylori$.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.question: Why is widespread community screening for H. pylori not recommended?
options: It significantly increases the risk of gastric cancer.
The extent of reduction in cancer risk from treatment is unknown.
All patients respond negatively to eradication therapy.
H. pylori is not associated with any serious conditions.
items.explanation: Widespread community screening for H. pylori is not recommended because the extent of reduction in cancer risk from treatment is unknown.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.question: What is a common reason to avoid treating asymptomatic populations with H. pylori?
options: The eradication therapy has no side effects.
Antibiotic resistance may emerge in incidentally carried bacteria.
All patients will develop GERD symptoms after treatment.
H. pylori eradication is universally supported for all conditions.
items.explanation: One reason to avoid treating asymptomatic populations with H. pylori is the potential emergence of antibiotic resistance in incidentally carried bacteria.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.statement: Helicobacter pylori colonization has been shown to offer protection against esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.explanation: Increasing evidence indicates that lifelong H. pylori colonization may offer some protection against complications of GERD, including esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: Treatment for Helicobacter pylori is beneficial in the treatment of gastric adenocarcinoma.
items.explanation: Treatment of H. pylori is of no benefit in the treatment of gastric adenocarcinoma.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: H. pylori infection is commonly acquired spontaneously in adulthood.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that spontaneous acquisition or loss of H. pylori in adulthood is uncommon.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Humans are the only important reservoir for H. pylori transmission.
items.explanation: The passage states that humans are the only important reservoir of H. pylori.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Magnification endoscopy correlates with microscopic findings and the presence of H. pylori.
items.explanation: Magnification endoscopy does correlate with microscopic findings or even with the presence of H. pylori, although it is insufficient for diagnosis.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Host genetic factors have been reproducibly associated with risk of acquisition of H. pylori infection.
items.explanation: There are currently no host genetic factors that have been reproducibly associated with the risk of acquiring H. pylori infection.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Gastric cancer often arises in the absence of H. pylori infection.
items.explanation: There has been a rise in gastric cancers centered on the gastric corpus occurring in younger adults, disproportionately in females, and apparently in the absence of H. pylori.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: CagA-positive H. pylori strains are not associated with t(11;18)(q21;q21)-positive gastric MALT lymphoma.
items.explanation: CagA-positive H. pylori strains have been significantly associated with the t(11;18)(q21;q21) positive gastric MALT lymphoma.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.statement: H. pylori eradication leads to symptom resolution in functional dyspepsia up to 15% more commonly than placebo treatment.
items.explanation: H. pylori eradication does lead to symptom resolution in functional dyspepsia more frequently compared to placebo.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.statement: The presence of H. pylori is a risk factor for the development of esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.explanation: H. pylori has an inverse relationship with esophageal adenocarcinoma, suggesting a protective role.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.statement: Microbiologic culture of H. pylori is the most sensitive test for diagnosing infection.
items.explanation: Microbiologic culture of H. pylori may be insensitive due to difficulty with isolation.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Serologic tests are used to monitor treatment success for H. pylori.
items.explanation: Serologic tests are not used to monitor treatment success due to the slow drop in antibody titers.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: H. pylori eradication is recommended for all patients with idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.
items.explanation: Eradication of H. pylori in conditions like idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura is not universally supported.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.statement: H. pylori eradication should be used for all cases of gastric low-grade B-cell MALT lymphoma.
items.explanation: Eradication is recommended in all cases of gastric low-grade B-cell MALT lymphoma due to the potential response rate.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.assertion: Helicobacter pylori has naturally colonized humans for at least 100,000 years.
items.reason: The organism's spiral shape and flagella render it motile in the mucus environment.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · Helicobacter pylori Infections | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.assertion: H. pylori colonization is accompanied by a considerable persistent local and systemic immune response.
items.reason: This response includes the production of antibodies and cell-mediated responses that are ineffective in clearing the bacterium due to its downregulation of the immune system.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the immune response is ineffective.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Diets high in antioxidants and vitamin C increase cancer risk.
items.reason: These diets are associated with increased disease risk due to their interaction with H. pylori.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; diets high in antioxidants and vitamin C are modestly protective, not increasing cancer risk.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: H. pylori is associated with a lower prevalence of GERD and esophageal adenocarcinoma.
items.reason: There is a temporal relationship between falling H. pylori prevalence and rising incidence of these conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.assertion: H. pylori eradication should always be prioritized in patients with peptic ulcer disease.
items.reason: Eradicating H. pylori changes the natural history of ulcer disease by reducing relapse rates.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, eradication is not always prioritized due to other factors like NSAID use.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.assertion: Noninvasive H. pylori testing is preferred if gastric cancer does not need to be excluded by endoscopy.
items.reason: The urea breath test and stool antigen test are accurate, convenient, and less invasive than endoscopic procedures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why noninvasive testing is preferred.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Biopsy-based tests can be used to assess treatment success immediately after antibiotic therapy.
items.reason: These tests depend on H. pylori load, and early suppression of bacterial numbers may lead to false-negative results.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because biopsy-based tests are unreliable if performed within 4 weeks of intercurrent treatment with antibiotics.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: H. pylori eradication reduces future gastric cancer risk.
items.reason: Eradication has been shown to slightly reduce future cancer incidence in some studies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reduction in cancer risk is not conclusively proven.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS

items.assertion: Monotherapy for H. pylori eradication is usually successful.
items.reason: Clinical failure of monotherapy has led to the development of multidrug regimens.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; monotherapy is not usually successful, leading to the use of multidrug regimens.
items.reference: Helicobacter pylori Infections · INDICATIONS | REGIMENS
